coredata.dc:title: Bio-inspired three-dimensional micro-nanoporous graphene for constructing Schottky junction and remarkably enhanced electrochemical detection
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Weiwei
He, Haiping
Ye, Zhizhen
Huang, Jingyun
coredata.dc:description: A facile and one-step transformation towards graphene using nickel foams as the substrate into a contact between semiconducting graphene (SG) and metal is developed for fabricating a SG/Ni Schottky junction. Graphene quantum dots were cut away via the electrochemical redox method and porous three-dimensional graphene with a bandgap was generated due to the abundant defects and dangling bonds. The constructed Schottky junction is the inner drives of accelerating charge transfer and thus results in significantly enhanced electrocatalytic effect. Bio-inspired porous graphene integrates micro-nano pores and functional groups, which greatly benefits to molecules separation and specific electrocatalysis. Macroscopic pores and numerous nanopores serve as “highways” for the rapid mass transport. Massive defects and rich dangling bonds act as “perceptrons” to reflect the charge distribution change. As a successful demonstration, the fabricated SG/Ni Schottky junction exhibits appreciable electro-oxidation towards H2O2 with an ultrahigh sensitivity of 4838 μA mM−1 cm-2 and an ultralow detection limit of 20 nM. This study puts forward an easy and practicable strategy to construct a Schottky junction, which is promisingly applied in other electrochemical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-inspired
Graphene
Micro-nano porous
Schottky
Junction
H2O2electro-oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the P3DG@NF electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of bio-inspired 3D graphene with micro-nano pores for the rapid mass transport and the electro-catalysis process of H2O2.
Morphology characterization. SEM images of 3DG@NF (a-b), P3DG@NF (c-d) with different magnifications and TEM images of GQDs (e), P3DG (f) after ultrasonic treatment.
XPS measurements. (a) Full spectrum and (b-f) high resolution spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, F 1 s, N 1 s, and P 2p.
Elemental characterization. (a) Representative local area of P3DG. (b-h) EDX elemental mapping images of C, N, O, F, P, and Ni.

Energy band measurements of P3DG and schematic of the SG/Ni Schottky junction. (a) UV–vis diffused reflection spectrum transformed by Kubelka–Munk function; (b) A typical He I UPS spectrum and detailed spectra of Fermi and cutoff region; (c-d) The respective energy level diagrams of nickel foam and p-Graphene; (e) Idealized equilibrium band diagram of the SG/Ni Schottky junction (empty circles represent holes).
CVs of (a) P3DG@NF obtained with different scan rates and (b) the corresponding redox peak current versus the scan rate.

(a) CVs in the absence and presence of 0.1 mM H2O2 the 3DG@NF (curve a and b, inset) and P3DG@NF (curve c and d) electrode. (b) Schematic of nanopores incision by BMIMPF6. (c) Typical steady-state response of electrocatalytic oxidation current by successive addition of H2O2. (d) The calibration curve of oxidation peak current versus H2O2 concentration.
Performance comparison of other published graphene based H2O2 sensors.

head.ce:title: Bio-inspired three-dimensional micro-nanoporous graphene for constructing Schottky junction and remarkably enhanced electrochemical detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Silicon Materials, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Silicon Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cyrus Tang Center for Sensor Materials and Applications, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cyrus Tang Center for Sensor Materials and Applications

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Silicon Materials, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Silicon Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and one-step transformation towards graphene using nickel foams as the substrate into a contact between semiconducting graphene (SG) and metal is developed for fabricating a SG/Ni Schottky junction. Graphene quantum dots were cut away via the electrochemical redox method and porous three-dimensional graphene with a bandgap was generated due to the abundant defects and dangling bonds. The constructed Schottky junction is the inner drives of accelerating charge transfer and thus results in significantly enhanced electrocatalytic effect. Bio-inspired porous graphene integrates micro-nano pores and functional groups, which greatly benefits to molecules separation and specific electrocatalysis. Macroscopic pores and numerous nanopores serve as “highways” for the rapid mass transport. Massive defects and rich dangling bonds act as “perceptrons” to reflect the charge distribution change. As a successful demonstration, the fabricated SG/Ni Schottky junction exhibits appreciable electro-oxidation towards H2O2 with an ultrahigh sensitivity of 4838 μA mM−1 cm-2 and an ultralow detection limit of 20 nM. This study puts forward an easy and practicable strategy to construct a Schottky junction, which is promisingly applied in other electrochemical research.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a two-dimensional (2D) monolayer of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. Due to the superiority in specific surface area, mechanical strength, thermal and electrical conductivity [1–3], tremendous effort towards graphene has been devoted to promoting the potentiality in various fields since its discovery in 2004. Benefiting from the advantages of mass production and convenient operation, chemical vapor deposition (CVD) has emerged to prepare large-area graphene films using transition metals as substrates [4,5]. Recently, 3D graphene architectures have been exploited and employed to integrate with the existing semiconductors for next-generation electronic and sensing devices. The studies have confirmed that graphene can contact conventional semiconductors to form rectifying junctions with excellent Schottky diodes characteristics [6–9]. Constructing graphene-semiconductor (G/S) junction is beneficial to better understand and fully develop the advantages of graphene integrated electronics.

The fabrication challenge is to establish an intimate G/S contact between the graphene and the semiconductor. Taking the example of graphene produced by CVD, it is inevitable to remove metal substrates using acid treatment to transfer graphene [10,11], which is complicated and environmentally unfriendly. Under the circumstance, the bottleneck is to design a novel Schottky junction while simultaneously preserving the metal substrate and obtaining the semiconducting graphene. The limitation has triggered great interest in the graphene energy band engineering, which opens a new door to realize an unusual semiconducting graphene-metal (SG/M) Schottky junction. Due to quantum confinement and edge effects, graphene can be transform into semiconductor with a wide bandgap by assembling the exfoliated bilayer graphene on a substrate [12] or curving 2D graphene into 1D nanoribbon [13,14]. Chemical doping, including functionalization on the graphene surface [15,16] and substitutional heteroatoms doping into the carbon lattice [17,18], is another route to obtain graphene with a bandgap. Combining advantages of quantum confinement effect and chemical doping to fabricate a SG/M Schottky junction, it has been a diligent direction. Du et al. offered a prediction that porosity of graphene brings about a direct band gap (3.2 eV) as early as 2010 [19]. Very recently, Barhoumi et al. present a theoretical study about chemically modified graphene by functional groups, bandgaps of which range from 4.5–6.5 eV [20].

Herein, inspired by the natural erosion landscape, we report a new strategy for facile construction of a semiconducting graphene-nickel (SG/Ni) junction by utilizing the metal substrate. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were electrochemically cut away using ionic liquid (1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (BMIMPF6) in acetonitrile) as the electrolyte, and a macroscopic 3D graphene network with plenty of nanopores using nickel foam as metal substrate was prepared (designated as P3DG@NF). Similar to the natural porous structure, 3D micro-nanoporous graphene not only has macroscopic pores for the rapid mass transport but also contains abundant nanopores to further separate molecules of varying sizes (Scheme 1 ). Massive defects and dangling bonds improve the sensitivity towards the charges distribution change and lead to the bandgap opening of graphene. The SG/Ni Schottky junction is established spontaneously after the nanopores incision of graphene and is considered the inner drives of accelerating the charge transfer speed. As a proof-of-concept demonstration, the fabricated SG/Ni Schottky junction was employed for the electro-oxidation of H2O2.

SEM images in Fig. 1 reveal the morphology change of 3D graphene grown on nickel foam before and after incising nanopores by electrochemical oxidation-reduction method. The initial graphene on nickel foam (3DG@NF) is smooth and consisted of 3D macro-porous skeleton with the average pore diameter about 200 μm (Fig. 1a). The magnified SEM images (Fig. 1b) show the surface remains intact except for some wrinkles due to the layers squeezing during the graphene deposition process. After electrochemical incision, the as-prepared porous graphene on nickel foam (P3DG@NF) gets coarse and creasing (Fig. 1c). Dense pores, ranged from a few nanometers to tens of nanometers, are uniformly distributed on the graphene backbones (Fig. 1d). As presented in Fig. 1e and f, TEM images reveal that the GQDs distributed in electrolyte with an average diameter of 3.0 nm and graphene backbones with irregular shaped pores after removing GQDs.

The surface chemistry of P3DG was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra (Fig. 2 a). The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s in Fig. 2b indicates three peaks located at 285, 286.8 and 287.9 eV, which are well attributed to covalent bond of CC, dangling bonds of C–N and C = O/C–F respectively. Fig. 2c shows peak-differentation-imitating of O 1 s at 531.2 and 532.6 eV, which demonstrates abundant O–P bonds linked to the pore defects. The observation in Fig. 2d confirms the massive existence of F 1 s, which further prove the effective pore incision by BMIMPF6. The high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s in Fig. 2e presents two distinct peaks at binding energy of 399 and 401.4 eV, corresponding to the N(Ⅰ) and N(Ⅲ) position on BMIM+. Fig. 2f displays existence of P 2p with two chemical states located at 133.8 and 136 eV, respectively. They can be further divided into four peaks, verifying the products of PO2F2− and PO3F2− due to the electrochemical reaction of PF6- during the pore incision process.

The phase differences of 3DG and P3DG was confirmed by XRD (Fig. S1). The peak at 2θ = 26.4° is ascribed to the (0 0 2) facet of graphene, which results from the graphene layers stacking during CVD. The other peaks at 42.2, 44.4, 54.5, 77.2° are in good agreement with (1 0 0), (1 0 1), (0 0 4), and (1 1 0) facets of graphite (JCPDS 41–1487). The broaden diffraction peaks of P3DG (olive curve) manifests the presence of numerous defects and the success of electrochemical incision. Given the XPS and XRD results, functional groups connection and structure defects lead to a bandgap of graphene. The Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis of P3DG@NF was further performed. The uniform distribution of the overall (Fig. 3 (b)), carbon (Fig. 3(c)), nitrogen (Fig. 3(d)), oxygen (Fig. 3(e)), fluorine (Fig. 3(f)), phosphorus (Fig. 3(g)) and nickel (Fig. 3(h)), reveals the successful construction of P3DG@NF.

The respective energy level diagrams and all detailed parameters of are shown in Fig. 4c and d, with the assumption of metal and semiconductor have common vacuum electron energy level. A metal/p-type semiconductor junction is formed spontaneously after the band opening of 3DG by incising nanopores (Fig. 4e). Because of the electron affinity of p-Graphene (ΦSG) is higher than nickel (ΦNi), the holes flow from NF to P3DG until the respective Fermi levels align at equilibrium. The charge transfer results in the formation of the space charge (or depletion) region at the semiconductor interface. The energy band of P3DG bends downwards and the hole barrier is constructed. Under a voltage bias, the alteration of the Schottky barrier height affects the junction current exponentially [24,25], and thus resulting in the outstanding detection sensitivity of the H2O2 electro-oxidation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the P3DG@NF electrode

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the electrocatalysis of the redox active P3DG@NF electrode, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were carried out with scan rates ranging from 10 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1 in 0.1 M NaOH. The cyclic voltammogram of NF in Fig. S3 displays a well-defined redox process with a quasi-reversible feature. The oxidation and reduction peak current increase with the scan rate over the potential range of 0.1 V to 0.5 V. The situation is similar for 3DG@NF, as shown in Fig. S4. The synergistic effect of porous graphene and SG/Ni Schottky junction causes a significantly improved electrochemical response (Fig. 5 a). The as-prepared P3DG@NF electrode exhibits much higher current signal, which is approximately 50 times as 3DG@NF and 8 times as NF. The anodic and cathodic peak current change linearly with the square root of the scan rate (Fig. 5b), indicating the redox reaction is surface confined [26,27].

The charge-transfer behavior was performed by EIS measurements in 0.1 M NaOH (Fig. S5). The Nyquist plots were obtained under open-circuit potential condition with the voltage amplitude of 5 mV at a frequency ranging from 0.01 Hz–100 kHz. The diameter of quasi-semicircular is equivalent to the resistance of electron transfer [28]. It is clear that nickel foam and P3DG exhibit the minimum and maximum charge transfer resistance. Compared with intact 3DG, complete quasi-semicircle of P3DG shows superior mass transfer ability due to the nanopores transport channels, which is in sharp contrast with 3DG@NF resulting from the coverage of graphene layers. The nearly complete quasi-semicircle with small diameter of P3DG@NF indicates low interfacial charge transfer resistance and excellent mass transfer property, making it great potential in electrochemical applications.

The electrocatalytic oxidation of H2O2 of the as-prepared P3DG@NF electrode was evaluated by cyclic voltammograms in 0.1 M NaOH. To minimize the impact of double layer capacitance, smaller scan rate was chosen for the comparison analysis [29,30]. The corresponding result in the presence and absence of H2O2 at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 is displayed in Fig. 6 a. The anodic peak current of the 3DG@NF electrode has a certain extent augment about 0.016 mA (curve a and b, inset), and the P3DG@NF electrode shows a distinct enhanced oxidation current about 0.36 mA (curve c and d) with the presence of H2O2. The peak current increment of the P3DG@NF electrode is 22.5 times as that of the 3DG@NF electrode. The striking contrast confirms the high electrocatalysis and fast charge transfer of P3DG@NF, which is the key in gaining electrochemical performance boost.

The nanopores incision process is illustrated in Fig. 6b. Plenty nanopores promote the efficiency of mass transport and charge transfer. Rich dangling bonds sensitively reflect the charge distribution change. Note that the similar energy band structure, just as solid state redox couples of oxide semiconductors [31,32], the corresponding catalytic mechanism towards H2O2 can be interpreted as follows:
(1)

O 2
where BMIM+ is positive1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium ion, and “ads” is the abbreviation for adsorption. First, one molecule of H2O2 was transported through nanopores and adsorbed by two molecules of BMIM+ (Eq. (1)). Next, the electrons transfer from the N atom of BMIM+ to the O atoms of H2O2, generating the weakness of OO bond and the oxidation of BMIM+ (Eq. (2)). Then, two OH− ions are released and transferred to current collector accompanied by the OO bond breaking and the electrons redistribution (Eq. (3)). In the end, BMIM2+ captures electron to produce BMIM+ (Eq. (4)), and OH− ions are oxidized to generate H2O2 and O2 (Eq. (5)). The step 1 and step 4 are considered as the rate determining steps [33]. It is a dynamic balance between electrons captured by BMIM2+ and maintaining the bending-down energy band the SG/Ni Schottky junction. That is, the Schottky junction acts like a pump with inexhaustibly supply of electrons to make the H2O2 electro-oxidation proceed swiftly.

Since fast and precise detection has been a prevailing trend in the sensing area, the analytical performance of the P3DG@NF electrode was therefore employed as an electro-oxidation H2O2 sensor. The steady-state amperometric response of the P3DG@NF electrode was investigated by successive addition of H2O2 at the constant potential of 0.4 V under stirring (Fig. 6c). Even ultralow concentration H2O2 of 500 nM renders regular increase of the electrocatalytic oxidation current (Fig. 6 c inset). The sensitive response exhibits a fast charge transfer and the steady-state current can be achieved within 2 s. Strong linearity (R2 = 0.999) with an ultrahigh sensitivity of 4838 μA mM−1 cm-2 is obtained up to the concentration of 1024 μM. The calibration curve of oxidation peak current versus the H2O2 concentration is plotted in Fig. 6e:
I
( m A ) = 6 . 44 ×
10
- 3
C + 0.0854
( μ m ) . The detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 20 nM according to the signal-to-noise of 3 [34].

Performance comparison of published graphene based H2O2 sensors with the as-prepared P3DG@NF electrode is shown in Table 1 . The one-step transformation from 3DG to P3DG is convenient and low cost comparing with the other reported methods. Though having no advantage in linear detection range, the P3DG@NF electrode demonstrates ultra-low detection limit and distinctly excellent sensitivity, which is multiple even dozens of times to most reported graphene based H2O2 sensors. The remarkable performance improvement can be attributed to the following points. (1) The P3DG@NF electrode directly to be a Schottky contact, not only utilizes nickel foam as the current collector but also guarantees the superior properties of graphene. (2) Countless micro-nano pores of the bio-inspired 3D graphene serve as the “highways” for rapid mass and charge transport channels, greatly shortening transmission distance from graphene layers to the current collector. (3) Rich dangling bonds at defects act as the “perceptrons” to sensitively reflect the charge distribution change. (4) The constructed SG/Ni Schottky junction is the inner drives of accelerating charge transfer and separation, and thus gaining significant sensing performance boost.

In conclusion, bio-inspired 3D micro-nano porous graphene was produced by incising nanopores towards CVD graphene by the electrochemical oxidation-reduction method. The whole preparation is simple, facile and low-cost. More importantly, a bandgap of graphene was successfully opened resulting from the abundant defects and dangling bonds. Rather than removing nickel foam by acid, we utilize the intimate contact between the metal substrate and porous graphene to construct the SG/Ni Schottky junction. The constructed SG/Ni Schottky junction is the inner drives of accelerating charge transfer and separation. Bio-inspired porous graphene integrates micro-nano pores and functional groups, greatly improving molecules separation and electrocatalysis. The synergistic effect of porous graphene and the Schottky junction leads to the remarkably enhanced electro-catalysis. As a successful demonstration, the fabricated SG/Ni Schottky junction exhibits efficient electro-oxidation towards H2O2 with ultrahigh sensitivity and ultralow detection limit. This study puts forward an easy and practicable strategy to prepare micro-nanoporous graphene and construct a Schottky junction, which is promisingly applied in fields of electrochemical research.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%, w/v), acetonitrile (CH3CN) and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (BMIMPF6) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company Limited (Shanghai, China). Nickel foams (NF) with a thickness of 1.2 mm and area density of 320 g cm−2 were purchased from Corun New Energy Co., Ltd. (Hunan, China). Ultrapure water of 18.2 MΩ produced by a Milli-Q purification system (Bedford, USA) was used throughout the experiments. All chemicals are of analytical grade and used without further purification.

CVD growth of graphene on nickel foam (3DG@NF) was prepared according to the previous study [ 5 ]. Electrochemical oxidation-reduction method was applied to incise nanopores on 3DG@NF by using BMIMPF6 in acetonitrile (2%, v/v) as the electrolyte and setting a constant voltage of 2.5 V (Chenhua CHI760e electrochemical workstation, platinum wire as the counter electrode, saturated calomel electrode as the reference). After then, P3DG@NF was taken out and rinsed with deionized water to remove the remnant GQDs. And the resultant electrolyte solution changed from colorless to yellow. The electrochemical cutting process can be summarized as follows: (1) PF6 − and BMIM+ intercalate the graphene layers under the applied electric voltage; (2) ions incision takes away GQDs and leads to structure defects and dangling bonds of 3DG@NF.

SEM analysis and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDX) were characterized by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (HITACHI SU8000 FE-SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Hitachi HT7700). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements over 2θ range of 10–80° were obtained by a D/Max-RBX-ray diffractomer (Rigaku, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on an Escalab 250Xi system (Thermo Scientific) using Mg Ka radiation. Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) diffused reflection spectrum was obtained by a Shimadzu UV3600 spectrophotometer. Electrochemical measurements were performed using a CHI760e electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai).

The electrochemical performance was recorded on a CHI760e electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system at room temperature. The as-prepared P3DG@NF (1.2 mm × 5 mm × 10 mm) or 3DG@NF (control group, 1.2 mm × 5 mm × 10 mm) electrode was served as the working electrode, using saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and platinum wire electrode (Ф = 1 mm) as the reference and counter electrodes respectively. Cyclic voltammograms were obtained with a potential window of 0.1 V to 0.5/0.6 V and amperometric measurements were carried out at a constant potential of 0.4 V. All the electrochemical measurements were carried out in 0.1 M NaOH aqueous solution with magnetic stirring.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Zhejiang Provincial Public Technology Research (LGG18E020001) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.104.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Weiwei Mao received his BE Degree in Department of Materials Science and Engineering at Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications in 2014. Since then, he has been studying for a PhD in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering of Zhejiang University. His current fields of interest focus on the design and fabrication of nanocomposites and electrochemical sensors.

Haiping He received his PhD Degree in Condensed Matter Physics from University of Science and Technology of China in 2004. At present, he is an associate professor of School of Materials Science and Engineering at Zhejiang University. His current research interests focus on optical and electrical properties of compound semiconductors, low-dimensional semiconductor nanostructures, quantum wells and luminescence of hybrid perovskites.

Zhizhen Ye received his PhD degree in 1987 from department of optical engineering at Zhejiang University. He is a professor of the State Key Laboratory of Silicon Materials and Cyrus Tang Center for Sensor Materials and Applications at Zhejiang University. His research interests mainly include the preparation and optical application of semiconductor thin films and nano-materials.

Jingyun Huang received his PhD degree in July 2000 from department of materials science and engineering at Zhejiang University. He is a professor of the State Key Laboratory of Silicon Materials and Cyrus Tang Center for Sensor Materials and Applications at Zhejiang University. He had been a visit scholar at Harvard University from 2004 to 2005 and at Georgia Institute of Technology from 2005 to 2006. His research interests mainly include the preparation and biosensor application of semiconductor thin films and nano-materials.
